heroHeadline: Historic US-Iran Proximity Talks Begin in Islamabad (Day 43); Hormuz Sea Mines Block Reopening; Iran Claims US Agreed to Frozen Assets — White House Denies; Markets Rebound Best Week Since November
heroSubtitle: The first direct US-Iran diplomatic engagement since the 1979 Islamic Revolution began April 11 in Islamabad, Pakistan, with VP Vance leading the US delegation and Iran's Ghalibaf + Araghchi leading Tehran's team in proximity talks via Pakistani mediation. The talks opened amid immediate disputes: Iran claims the US agreed to release frozen assets held in Qatar as a precondition — the White House flatly denied this. Iran's red lines include control of the Strait of Hormuz, a Lebanon ceasefire, and frozen asset release. The Strait remains effectively closed: Iran admitted sea mines are in the waterway, and US officials told Haaretz Iran cannot remove them. Despite fragility, the ceasefire held through Day 43. US markets recovered strongly — the S&P 500 posted its best week since November (+3.6%), though fell modestly on April 10 on renewed threats from Trump.
footerNote: Data sourced from White House, Federal Register, executive orders, court records, Al Jazeera, CNN, Reuters, AP, SCOTUSblog, NPR, BLS, Peterson Institute for International Economics, Tax Foundation, CFR, EIA, Treasury, Goldman Sachs, Federal Reserve, OECD, Cato Institute, Penn Wharton Budget Model, Bloomberg, CNBC, Axios, NBC News, PBS NewsHour.
